concerns.text: The plan proposes correlating SO tag trends with GitHub stars/NPM downloads to 'validate that tag frequency changes reflect actual adoption' (FR-007). This assumes a direct causal link or high construct validity between 'GitHub stars' and 'technology adoption' which is not established. Stars measure repository interest, not necessarily usage or adoption in production. Using this as a validation metric for 'adoption' risks construct validity failure. The plan must explicitly acknowledge this limitation or define a more direct proxy for adoption if available.

The plan uses Pearson correlation for co-occurrence clustering. Co-occurrence in SO posts is a binary or count relationship (tags on same post), not necessarily a linear continuous variable suitable for Pearson correlation. While often used, Pearson assumes linearity and normality which may not hold for sparse co-occurrence matrices. The plan should justify why Pearson is appropriate over Jaccard similarity or other metrics better suited for binary co-occurrence data, or acknowledge the potential distortion in cluster formation.

The plan applies Mann-Kendall to monthly aggregated data (n=108). While n>10 is sufficient for the test, the plan does not address the issue of serial correlation (autocorrelation) in time series data. Mann-Kendall assumes independent observations. If the monthly tag frequencies are highly autocorrelated (which is likely for technology trends), the standard Mann-Kendall test will overestimate significance (Type I error). The plan must specify a pre-whitening procedure or use a modified Mann-Kendall test that accounts for autocorrelation to ensure causal validity of the trend significance claims.
